title: Listening - Part 4: A Rooftop Vegetable Garden
description: You will hear part of a radio interview with a woman called Aisha, who organises a rooftop garden in her city. For questions 24–30, choose the correct answer (A, B or C).

content.audio_script: Presenter: Today I’m meeting Aisha Rahman, who manages a vegetable garden on the roof of an apartment block. Aisha, how did the project begin? || Aisha: The building had a large flat roof which residents only used for storing old chairs. Meanwhile, the café on the ground floor bought herbs from a supplier miles away. [24]I suggested using part of that unused roof to grow fresh herbs for the café and inviting residents to help[/24]. The owner agreed to a small trial, and it gradually grew. || Presenter: Is growing plants high above the street more difficult than gardening on the ground? || Aisha: In some ways it’s easier because we don’t have heavy soil or many weeds. People often ask about air pollution, which we monitor carefully, but that hasn’t been our greatest issue. [25]The strongest wind dries plants out much faster than we expected, so protecting them and checking water levels is essential[/25]. || Presenter: How much time do residents need to give? || Aisha: Some residents love gardening every weekend, while others can only contribute occasionally. We didn’t want people to feel guilty or excluded. [26]We created short weekly sessions with clear jobs, so someone can help for twenty minutes and still make a useful contribution[/26]. This works especially well for residents with busy jobs or young children. || Presenter: How do you manage water on a roof? || Aisha: Carrying watering cans upstairs would be exhausting and wasteful. We installed containers that collect rain from a small covered area. [27]Using stored rainwater means we need much less water from the building's main supply during the summer[/27]. In long dry periods, we still need extra, but the system is a big help. || Presenter: What has grown particularly well? || Aisha: Herbs were our original aim, and they are reliable. We also tried tomatoes, beans and strawberries. [28]The strawberries were a surprise because they produced much more fruit than any of us expected in such an exposed place[/28]. Children in the building became very good at checking when they were ready to pick. || Presenter: Has the garden affected the residents in other ways? || Aisha: Absolutely. Fresh food is lovely, but the social change matters more. [29]People who used to pass silently in the lift now spend time together on the roof and know one another by name[/29]. One elderly resident says the sessions are the part of the week she looks forward to most. || Presenter: Are you planning to make the garden larger? || Aisha: Space is almost full, so expanding here isn’t realistic. Instead, [30]I want to train residents from nearby buildings so they can start similar gardens on their own roofs[/30]. A network of small gardens would have a greater effect than one enormous project. || Presenter: Aisha, thanks for joining us. || Aisha: Thank you.

task.title: Part 4
task.instruction: For questions 24–30, choose the correct answer (A, B or C).

questions.question: Why did Aisha first suggest creating the roof garden?

options.A: Residents had asked for somewhere quiet to sit.
options.B: The space could be used to grow herbs locally.
options.C: The building needed to reduce the weight on its roof.

questions.explanation: Aisha proposed growing fresh herbs for the café in unused rooftop space.

questions.question: What has been the main difficulty in growing plants on the roof?

options.A: Plants become dry because of the wind.
options.B: Pollution makes the produce unsafe to eat.
options.C: Weeds grow more rapidly than on the ground.

questions.explanation: Strong wind causes plants to dry out more quickly than expected.

questions.question: How does Aisha encourage more residents to participate?

options.A: She asks each resident to care for one plant.
options.C: She offers useful tasks that can be done quickly.

questions.explanation: Short sessions let people contribute even if they only have twenty minutes.

questions.question: What is the benefit of the garden's water system?

options.A: It reduces the use of water from the building.
options.B: It prevents plants receiving too much rainwater.
options.C: It supplies water to gardens in neighbouring buildings.

questions.explanation: Collected rainwater means less water is taken from the main supply.

questions.question: What surprised Aisha about the plants?

options.A: The herbs grew more slowly than expected.
options.B: The strawberry plants were especially productive.
options.C: Children preferred growing beans to fruit.

questions.explanation: She was surprised that the strawberries produced a large amount of fruit.

questions.question: What does Aisha consider an important result of the project?

options.A: Residents have started eating healthier meals.
options.B: The café has attracted more customers.

questions.explanation: Residents who previously did not speak now spend time together and know each other.

questions.question: What does Aisha hope to do next?

options.A: Help people create gardens on other buildings.
options.B: Find more space on the existing roof.
options.C: Sell vegetables to other local cafés.

questions.explanation: She wants to train residents from nearby apartment blocks to start their own gardens.
